question.text: A call option with a strike price of Rs. 17,500 when the Nifty spot is at Rs. 18,000 is said to be:

answer.text: In-the-Money (ITM) because the spot price is above the strike price
Out-of-the-Money (OTM) because the spot price is above the strike price
At-the-Money (ATM) because the difference is only 500 points
Deep Out-of-the-Money because the gap between spot and strike is significant

explanation.text:
**The Logic:** For a CALL option: ITM when Spot > Strike. Here Spot (18,000) > Strike (17,500), so it is ITM. - Call ITM: S > X (spot above strike) - Call ATM: S ≈ X (spot near strike) - Call OTM: S < X (spot below strike)
**The Trap:** Moneyness rules are OPPOSITE for puts. For puts: ITM when S < X.
**Cross-Exam Context:** Moneyness determines intrinsic value and is heavily tested in calculation questions.
**Distributor's Pro-Tip:** Call ITM: S > X. Put ITM: S < X. ATM: S ≈ X.
